text: Shortly after dawn, the cloudy sky promised more bad weather, and the rise of the sea against the medieval wall made the whole dig feel accidental, even doomed. Our team of archaeology students had to recognize that the project stood on the brink of disaster. The persistence of one entrant, a resolute woman who could memorize every longitude and meteorology chart off-hand, kept us going. She would not default on her promise, nor revert to despair. "Optimism is a kind of potion," she remarked with quiet daring, "and stupidity its allergy." Who could contradict her? She arranged the disposal of the loose pottery shards with optimum care, smelling each for the faint smell of old glue, and sorting them into a neat tetrad on a flat cork board. An owl watched from a shadowy fig tree as the morning light began to emerge. The legendary catastrophe that had buried this town was no idyll: an explosion of the nearby volcano, accordingly, had crumpled its octagon temple like paper. We could still see the dent where a falling beam had struck, the eddy of ash that froze mid-swirl, and a remnant of a cherry-wood saw left by a worker who fled in desperation. There would be no congratulation, no easy deduction, until the correlation between the layers was proven. A speculator had once tried to commercialize the site, his fraud a kind of burglary against history; we opposed him at the rendezvous point and forced him to default. The increase in tourists soon brought distraction. One man, full of make-up and chic clothes, kept a mug of alcohol at his side and would haggle over every artifact; his short-weight scales and l/c documents were plainly suspect, and a prohibition was quickly issued against him. He aggravated everyone, yet we did not let him disturb the work. By till and trowel we proceeded, our thinking sharpened by hardship. Quite suddenly the persistence paid off: an entrant brushed aside the dust and a carved face seemed to convince us, as if to consultation, that the ancient Jew, the dictatorship that ruled here, the long ordeal of its people, were all real. The enhancement of our records was immediate. We felt a flame of pride. Specialized scholars would study the find extensively, and any conceivable doubt about the town's population would, at last, be laid to rest. Working outdoors at speed, we would not aggravate the fragile site nor crumple a single record; archaeology was our true occupation, and we would oppose any destructive scenario. With calm poise I made one final remark, refusing to implant false hope: no handcuff of regret, no rotten potato of doubt, would slow us now.
translation: 黎明后不久，多云的天空预示着更坏的天气，海水拍打中世纪城墙的上涨让整个发掘现场都显得偶然，甚至注定失败。我们这支考古学学生队伍不得不承认，项目正处于灾难的边缘。一位参赛者的坚持支撑着我们前行，她是个果断的女人，能不假思索地记住每一条经度和气象图表。她不会拖欠她的承诺，也不会重陷绝望。"乐观是一种药剂，"她带着平静的大胆评论道，"而愚蠢则是它的过敏源。"谁能反驳她呢？她以最适宜的细心安排了那些松散陶片的处理，逐一嗅闻每片是否带着陈年胶水的淡淡气味，并把它们分类摆成整齐的一组四件，放在一块平整的软木板上。一只猫头鹰从一棵幽暗的无花果树上注视着，晨光开始浮现。掩埋这座城镇的传奇大灾难绝非田园牧歌：附近火山的一次爆发，因此，把它的八角形神庙像纸一样压皱了。我们仍能看到落下的横梁砸出的凹痕，凝固在半旋中的灰烬漩涡，以及一名绝望逃离的工人留下的樱桃木锯子的残余。在各层之间的相互关系被证实之前，不会有任何祝贺，也不会有轻易的推断。一个投机商曾试图把这处遗址商业化，他的欺诈是对历史的一种盗窃；我们在集合地点反对他，迫使他违约退出。游客的增加很快带来了分心。有个男人，浓妆艳抹、衣着别致，身边总放着一大杯酒，对每件文物都讨价还价；他短斤少两的秤和他的信用证文件明显可疑，针对他的禁令很快就发布了。他使每个人都恼火，但我们没让他扰乱工作。我们靠着直到天黑的劳作和泥铲推进，思想在艰苦中变得敏锐。相当突然地，坚持得到了回报：一位参赛者拂去尘土，一张雕刻的面孔仿佛在向我们咨询般使我们信服——古代的犹太人、曾统治这里的独裁政权、它的人民漫长的严酷考验，全都是真实的。我们记录的提升立竿见影。我们感到一阵自豪的火焰。专门的学者将广泛研究这一发现，而关于这座城镇人口的任何可以想象的疑问，终将得以平息。在户外快速工作，我们不会使这脆弱的遗址恶化，也不会弄皱任何一份记录；考古是我们真正的职业，我们会反对任何破坏性的设想。我以镇定的姿态作了最后一句评论，拒绝灌输虚假的希望：没有悔恨的手铐，没有疑虑这颗烂土豆，能拖慢我们的脚步。
